description: A designed warning poster from USCIS. The top half is a color stock photograph of an interview scene: a Black man in a grey button-down shirt sits at a table speaking earnestly to another person seen from behind (back of a head, white shirt), evoking a USCIS officer interviewing an applicant in a bright office setting. The bottom half is a solid dark navy panel containing, centered, the full-color U.S. Department of Homeland Security seal above the agency wordmark 'U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services' in light grey serif text. Below that, in large bold pale-yellow sans-serif type, the headline warning: 'Don’t intentionally misrepresent facts during your interview', and beneath it in white: 'You can lose your status and be removed from the U.S.' The visual style is clean and official (photo-over-color-block layout, agency seal, two-tier typographic hierarchy). The propaganda/messaging technique is deterrence: a direct second-person warning paired with a relatable interview image to threaten loss of status and removal for misrepresentation, tied to the tweet's story about a former Haitian mayor convicted of visa fraud. Target audience: immigration applicants/interviewees and the public.
notable_text: Don’t intentionally misrepresent facts during your interview; You can lose your status and be removed from the U.S.
